DayGlassBar
How it works Features Download FAQ GitHub
Free · Windows & macOS

DayGlassBar

See your day drain — quietly.

An ambient bar that lives at the edge of your screen and shows the time left in your day. No numbers, no colors changing, no alarms — just a thin sliver of light that shrinks as the day goes by.

Download View on GitHub

Latest release · Early days — expect a few rough edges.

schedule.js — DayGlassBar
1234567
// remaining time, quietly
const day = { start: '09:00', end: '17:00' };
const lunch = ['12:00', '13:00'];
 
function left(now) {
  return day.end - now;
}
Today
10:00Standup
12:00Lunch
14:001:1 — Sam
16:00Review
16:15
9:00 17:00
16:15 45m left

Live demo: the fill is the time left in your day — hover expands it.

How it works

1

Set your day

Pick the segment (e.g. 9:00–17:00) and which days are on. Breaks like lunch are greyed out.

2

Catch it at a glance

A thin bar sits on your screen's edge and fills only the time remaining. As the day passes, the fill shrinks — that's it.

3

Hover for detail

Rest the cursor on the bar and it widens to show start, end, now and time left — then slips back. It's always click-through, so it never steals your clicks.

Features

Ambient by design

Lives at the edge of your screen — pin it to any side: top, bottom, left or right. No countdowns, no blinking, no notifications; only the fill length changes.

Calendar integration

Connect Google Calendar, or read classic Outlook locally. Events inside your day appear as colored bands; hover for the title.

Click-through & always on top

Sits above everything but ignores the mouse — the work underneath is never blocked, even while expanded.

Download

Latest release, built automatically by GitHub Actions.

Windows

Windows 10 / 11 (64-bit)

Installer (.exe) Portable (.exe)

macOS

Apple Silicon

Download (.dmg)
Heads up: the app isn't code-signed yet. On Windows, the SmartScreen prompt may appear — click More info → Run anyway. On macOS, right-click the app → Open (or run xattr -dr com.apple.quarantine on it).

All releases & release notes →

FAQ

Does it turn red or blink as the day winds down?

No. "Rush" cues are intentionally left out — only the fill length shrinks, and the color stays constant. The whole point is to nudge, not rush.

Is it a Pomodoro timer? Does it notify me?

No. Pomodoro, sounds and notifications are out of scope. It's a display-only tool for feeling how much of your day is left.

Can it show my calendar?

Yes. Connect Google Calendar (OAuth), or read classic Outlook locally on Windows — each with its own on/off and color. Events inside your day appear as colored bands; hover to see the title. Outlook's cloud API is currently unsupported.

Will it work with my company's Outlook?

Use the local connection with classic Outlook installed on that PC — no sign-in or admin approval needed, and it reads work-account events too. (The "new Outlook" / web-only setups aren't supported for local reading.)

Can I subscribe to a public ICS calendar URL?

No. It was implemented once and removed: ICS feeds are cached by the provider for hours, so they can't follow mid-day changes fast enough.

Is it free? What about my data?

It's free to download. Your settings live in a local JSON; calendar sign-in tokens are encrypted on your device and never included in exports. Events are only fetched for display — nothing is sent to a cloud of ours.

If you find it useful, a small tip on Ko-fi is hugely appreciated — but it's completely optional. ♡

DayGlassBar

A nudge, not a nag.
